```json
  "active_set": ["R1.1", "R1.2", "R1.3", "R1.6", "RX.1", "RX.2", "RX.3", "RX.4", "RX.5"],
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "The thesis has a serious coherence fault: GPT-3 is described as 'the current frontier' and 'state of the art' while the pilot ran in late 2024, by which time GPT-4 and several successor models had long been released. Strengthening requires either updating the target model or reframing the contribution claim so it survives the anachronism. The gap statement (non-experts failing at detection) and the contribution claim (calibration as a fix) are both present but underpowered.",
    "R1.2": "The jump from a 6.7-point accuracy gain to 'human evaluation is recoverable' is asserted rather than argued. The causal mechanism — why five labeled examples shift annotator priors enough to matter — has no logical scaffolding. The 'AI-tell prior' construct is introduced and then used as an explanatory device without being independently validated.",
    "R1.3": "Results are stated (51.4%, 58.1%, p<0.01) without per-condition n, effect size, or confidence intervals. The generalization claim in the final sentence — that the calibration protocol applies to systems 'readers will most likely deploy' — is not grounded in any cross-model evidence and overstretches what the single-model design supports.",
    "R1.6": "'Make this stronger' in a writing context routinely targets prose impact: topic-sentence force, precision of key terms (e.g., 'modest but statistically significant' hedges in both directions simultaneously), and the rhetorical arc of the conclusion. All of these have room for improvement here."
  },
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "Does 'stronger' mean primarily improving the prose and rhetorical force of the writing (sentence-level precision, impact, clarity), or primarily shoring up the scientific argument (fixing logical gaps, evidence-claim grounding, and the conceptual coherence of the GPT-3-as-frontier framing)?"
}
```

rationale.R1.1: The thesis has a serious coherence fault: GPT-3 is described as 'the current frontier' and 'state of the art' while the pilot ran in late 2024, by which time GPT-4 and several successor models had long been released. Strengthening requires either updating the target model or reframing the contribution claim so it survives the anachronism. The gap statement (non-experts failing at detection) and the contribution claim (calibration as a fix) are both present but underpowered.
rationale.R1.2: The jump from a 6.7-point accuracy gain to 'human evaluation is recoverable' is asserted rather than argued. The causal mechanism — why five labeled examples shift annotator priors enough to matter — has no logical scaffolding. The 'AI-tell prior' construct is introduced and then used as an explanatory device without being independently validated.
rationale.R1.3: Results are stated (51.4%, 58.1%, p<0.01) without per-condition n, effect size, or confidence intervals. The generalization claim in the final sentence — that the calibration protocol applies to systems 'readers will most likely deploy' — is not grounded in any cross-model evidence and overstretches what the single-model design supports.
rationale.R1.6: 'Make this stronger' in a writing context routinely targets prose impact: topic-sentence force, precision of key terms (e.g., 'modest but statistically significant' hedges in both directions simultaneously), and the rhetorical arc of the conclusion. All of these have room for improvement here.

parsed.clarification_question: Does 'stronger' mean primarily improving the prose and rhetorical force of the writing (sentence-level precision, impact, clarity), or primarily shoring up the scientific argument (fixing logical gaps, evidence-claim grounding, and the conceptual coherence of the GPT-3-as-frontier framing)?
